request.question: Which game can be played by more than 2 players at a time, Game of the Generals or Ludo?

title: Game of the Generals
Rampage: Total Destruction
Ludo (board game)
Sholo Guti
Michael George Dupée
Jeep Thrills
Gundam War Collectible Card Game
Nd game
Monster Monpiece
White Men Can't Jump (video game)
sentences: The Game of the Generals, also called GG as it is most fondly called, or simply The Generals, is an educational war game invented in the Philippines by Sofronio H. Pasola, Jr. in 1970.
Its Filipino name is "Salpakan."
It can be played within twenty to thirty minutes.

It is designed for two players, each controlling an army, and a neutral arbiter (sometimes called a referee or an adjutant) to decide the results of "challenges" between opposing playing pieces, that like playing cards, have their identities hidden from the opponent.
Rampage: Total Destruction is the latest sequel to the "Rampage" arcade game produced by Midway Games.
The game was released on April 24, 2006 in North America for the GameCube (1-4 players) and PlayStation 2 (1-2 players).
It was also released on November 14, 2006 in North America for the Wii (1-4 players).
Ludo , (from Latin "ludo", "I play") is a strategy board game for two to four players, in which the players race their four tokens from start to finish according to the rolls of a single die.
Like other cross and circle games, Ludo is derived from the Indian game Pachisi, but simpler.
The game and its variations are popular in many countries and under various names.
Sholo Guti is a Checker game like Chess played between 2 players.
It is specially famous in Bangladesh and some of the Indian regions.
Local people of the village passes their leisure time by playing it.
They draw the cort of this game on the ground and uses stones as pawns.

Michael George "Mike" Dupée (born October 23, 1966) is an American game show contestant and author who played on the U.S. syndicated game show "Jeopardy!" At the time that he won the 1996 Jeopardy! Tournament of Champions, he was the 4th biggest winner in Jeopardy! history.
Between 1996 and 2005, Dupée was one of only 2 players who competed on "Jeopardy!" without ever losing a game.
Jeep Thrills is a video game for the PlayStation 2 and Wii consoles.
It was developed by Game Sauce and published by DSI Games.
It can be played by 1 or 2 players.
Gundam War: Mobile Suit Gundam the Card Game also known simply as Gundam War is a collectible card game based on the Gundam anime series produced by Bandai.
Players can simulate battles in the anime series.
The game is designed for 2 players, though there may be different fan-created multiplayer rules.
This game is sometimes confused with the Gundam M.S. War Trading Card Game, since both are published by Bandai and are based on the Gundam series.
A n game (or n game) is a generalization of the game Tic-tac-toe to higher dimensions.
It is a game played on a n hypercube with 2 players.
If one player creates a line of length n of their symbol (X or O) they win the game.
However, if all n spaces are filled then the game is a draw.
Tic-tac-toe is the game where n equals 3 and d equals 2 (3,2).
Qubic is the (4,3) game.
The (n>0,0) or (1,1) games are trivially won by the first player as there is only one space (n=1 and 1=1).
The game with d=1, n>1 cannot be won because an opponents piece will block the one-dimensional line.
Monster Monpiece (Japanese: 限界凸騎 モンスターモンピース , Hepburn: Genkai Tokki Monsutā Monpīsu , lit.
"Unlikely Knight: Monster Monpiece") is a card battle video game developed by Compile Heart for the PlayStation Vita.
The game re-imagines mythological creatures and monsters, such as unicorns and minotaurs, as girls and women created by more than 50 different artists.
The game allows multiplayer card battles online for 2 players.
The game is based around battling with four types of cards, not all of which are monsters.
"Monster Monpiece" was released on January 24, 2013 in Japan, and an English localization was released by Idea Factory International in May 2014.
White Men Can't Jump is a video game released for the Atari Jaguar in 1995 by High Voltage Software, loosely based on the 1992 20th Century Fox movie of the same name.
As in the movie, players play games of two-on-two street basketball.
It was bundled with the Team Tap Adaptor, which allowed three or four players to play in tandem despite the game box saying "1 or 2 players".
Due to its poor graphics, among other factors, it has been hailed as one of the worst games of all time by some critics.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: Which game can be played by more than 2 players at a time, Game of the Generals or Ludo?
hop1_docs: Ludo (board game) | Ludo , (from Latin "ludo", "I play") is a strategy board game for two to four players, in which the players race their four tokens from start to finish according to the rolls of a single die.  Like other cross and circle games, Ludo is derived from the Indian game Pachisi, but simpler.  The game and its variations are popular in many countries and under various names.

Calculation (card game) | Calculation (also known as Broken Intervals and as Hopscotch) is a solitaire card game played with a standard pack of 52 cards.  It offers more scope for skill than many similar games; a skilled player can win Calculation more than half of the time when "normal play" can allow winning 1 in 5 times.

Multiplayer video game | video game in which more than one person can play in the same game environment at the same time.  Video games are often single-player activities, putting the player against preprogrammed challenges or AI-controlled opponents (which lack the flexibility of human thought).  Multiplayer games allow players interaction with other individuals in partnership, competition or rivalry, providing them with social communication absent from single-player games.  In multiplayer games, players may compete against two (or more) human contestants, work cooperatively with a human partner to achieve a common goal, supervise other players' activity, co-op, and objective-based modes assaulting (or defending) a control point.  Multiplayer games typically require players to share the resources of a single game system or use networking technology to play together over a greater distance.

Community-controlled game | A community-controlled game (CCG), is any video game featuring a single avatar that is controlled by more than one person.  As the definition of a CCG refers to the way a game is played, rather than aspects of the game itself, a CCG can be played in any genre.  However, according to CCG streamer Zach Gerlock, Role-Playing Games (RPGs) are the most compatible, because this genre generally does not require reaction-based input.  A CCG will intake commands from multiple players and will issue them to the avatar.  However, as players cannot predict what other players will input, the movements and actions of the avatar can be incredibly erratic depending on the game's input mode.

Supremacy 1914 | Supremacy 1914 is a player real-time strategy browser game created by Bytro Labs and published by Bigpoint Games in which the player manages one of the countries in the world during World War I.  The player competes with countries controlled by other players playing the same game, and with countries controlled by the computer.  Resource management, province upgrades, and alliances play key roles in the game, in addition to the conquest aspect.  The main view of the game is a map that can be zoomed in and out, but there is also a province view, which lets you see the upgrades to that province in a more realistic view than the list format that many games of this type have.

Sandlot ball | Sandlot ball or sandlot baseball is a North American game of children and adolescents that generally follows the basic rules of baseball.  More specific rules can be set for games and may vary each time the game is played.  These rules are usually agreed upon before the game begins by teams of young boys or girls usually from the same neighborhood.  The word sandlot refers to the makeshift field, which could be nothing more than an empty piece of land in the area composed of grass, dirt, or sand that is big enough to facilitate the game.  Objects used in playing the game can be improvised to take the place of bases, balls, or bats if they are unavailable.

Pick-up game | In sports, a pick-up game is a game that has been spontaneously started by a group of players.  Players are generally invited to show up beforehand, but unlike exhibition games there is no sense of obligation or commitment to play.  Pick-up games usually lack officials and referees, which makes them more disorganized and less structured than regular games but the total number of players in such games globally is likely to be greater than the number playing in formal competitions and leagues.
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Ludo — "Ludo (board game)" (passage 1): "strategy board game for two to four players" (quote).
  - Game of the Generals — no mention in supplied passages.

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - Missing page/facts for Game of the Generals; no player-count or rules provided for that game in the passages.

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - Exact player count (minimum/maximum or typical number of players) for Game of the Generals, or an explicit statement whether it can be played by more than 2 players.

- retrieval_direction (priority):
  1. Retrieve "Game of the Generals" Wikipedia page or the page titled "Game of the Generals (board game)".
  2. Find "Game of the Generals rules number of players" or "How many players play Game of the Generals".
  3. Look for official rules or rulebook / authoritative descriptions (e.g., rule PDF, museum or game sites) that state player count.
response.hop2_query: Game of the Generals number of players
hop2_docs: Pick-up game | In sports, a pick-up game is a game that has been spontaneously started by a group of players.  Players are generally invited to show up beforehand, but unlike exhibition games there is no sense of obligation or commitment to play.  Pick-up games usually lack officials and referees, which makes them more disorganized and less structured than regular games but the total number of players in such games globally is likely to be greater than the number playing in formal competitions and leagues.

Quantum refereed game | Quantum refereed game in quantum information processing is a class of games in the general theory of quantum games.  It is played between two players, Alice and Bob, and arbitrated by a referee.  The referee outputs the pay-off for the players after interacting with them for a fixed number of rounds, while exchanging quantum information.

Generals Order | Generals Order (GO), or 将军令, is a strategy trading card game developed by Strategy Entertainment Pte Ltd of Singapore and introduced in 2007.  Generals Order is the first Chinese strategy trading card game developed by a Singapore company and despite its humble beginnings, has a player base in the hundreds and growing .  Generals Order can be played by two or more players each using a deck of printed cards.  The number of cards in one deck can vary from forty to sixty cards.

Rugby union positions | In the game of rugby union, there are 15 players on each team, comprising eight forwards (numbered 1–8) and seven backs (numbered 9–15).  In addition, there may be up to eight replacement players "on the bench".  Jersey numbers 16–23 differentiate them.  Players are not restricted to any single position on the field, although they generally specialise in just one or two that suit their skills and body types.  Players that specialise in over three positions are called "utility players".

Games played | Games played (most often abbreviated as G or GP) is a statistic used in team sports to indicate the total number of games in which a player has participated (in any capacity); the statistic is generally applied irrespective of whatever portion of the game is contested.

N-player game | In game theory, an "n"-player game is a game which is well defined for any number of players.  This is usually used in contrast to standard 2-player games that are only specified for two players.  In defining "n"-player games, game theorists usually provide a definition that allow for any (finite) number of players.

Immersive sim | An immersive sim (simulation) is a video game genre with games that are typically played from a first-person perspective, and include a number of different elements borrowing from computer role-playing games, stealth games, survival horror and platform games.  These elements are combined along with a compelling environmental setting as to give the player a stronger feeling of control within the game (the sense of immersion), and to encourage emergent gameplay, where the player has the ability to complete game objectives through a variety of options.  These games generally feature open-ended gameplay, allowing the player to progress in any order, often by pursue side missions alongside various main story missions.  Immersive sims are generally compared to the first titles in the game series "Thief", "System Shock", and "Deus Ex", known as the earliest and critically acclaimed titles in the genre.
